question: Did the review adhere to pre-defined objectives and eligibility criteria?
answers: nan

question: Were the eligibility crieria appropriate for the review question?
answers:
Herein, we seek to consolidate current understandings of the disease

Exclusion criteria denied the inclusion of articles not written in English, conference abstracts, studies not performed in primary human subjects, and studies with inadequate patient data. Articles discussing cases of allergic contact dermatitis in response to shiitake mushroom exposure were not included. Additionally, cases of systemic symptoms (i.e. asthma or gastrointestinal distress) without dermatologic manifestations were not included.

question: Did the search include an appropriate range of databases/ electronic sources for published and unpublished reports?
answers: The National Library of Medicine's PubMed database was systematically searched (to July 2015) for articles pertaining to the topic of shiitake dermatitis. The search terms used were "shiitake" with "dermatitis" and "toxicoderma". Search results were cross-checked with the Web of Science database using the same search terms.

question: Were methods additional to database searching used to identify relevant reports?
answers: nan

question: Were the terms and structure of the search strategy likely to retrieve as many eligible studies as possible?
answers: The search terms used were "shiitake" with "dermatitis" and "toxicoderma

question: Were search strategy restrictions based on date, publication format, or language appropriate?
answers:
The search terms used were "shiitake" with "dermatitis" and "toxicoderma". Search results were cross-checked with the Web of Science database using the same search terms. Article titles and abstracts were screened, and the full texts of all possibly relevant articles were retrieved to allow their evaluation for
inclusion and the extraction of data. Exclusion criteria
denied the inclusion of articles not written in English,
conference abstracts, studies not performed in primary human
subjects, and studies with inadequate patient data. Articles
discussing cases of allergic contact dermatitis in response to
shiitake mushroom exposure were not included. Additionally,
cases of systemic symptoms (i.e. asthma or gastrointestinal
distress) without dermatologic manifestations were not
included.

answers: nan

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in data collection?
answers: nan

question: Were sufficient study characteristics considered for both review authors and readers to be able to interpret the results?
answers:
Etiology
All included cases resulted after the oral consumption of shiitake mushroom in various forms, including raw (n = 26) and
lightly or undercooked (including in soups and stir fries, n = 10).
In three cases, the subjects had not experienced any reactions
to the consumption of shiitake mushrooms prior to the reported
incident: one case had previously consumed roasted mushrooms,
and another reported no reaction after consuming shiitake
mushrooms as recently as 2 weeks previously.6,28 Two
cases reported recurring symptoms before diagnosis (at 1 year
and 16 years, respectively).7,9 One subject reported the daily
consumption of raw mushrooms for 2 weeks prior to onset.33
No reports investigated genetic predisposition or familial patterns
of shiitake dermatitis (Table 2).
Demographics
The mean patient age was 44.58 years (range: 25-86 years).
Incidence was more common in males at a 3 : 1 ratio (38
males, 12 females). The highest number of cases was reported
in France, as documented in a systematic retrospective study
on shiitake dermatitis (n = 15) carried out through the French
Poison Control Centers.22 Other countries with high numbers of
cases included the USA (n = 9), Germany (n = 8), and the UK
(n = 6). Asian cases are likely to be under-represented because
non-English-language articles were excluded from this review
(Table 1).
Clinical presentation
The classically associated linear flagellate dermatitis was present
in 98% of cases. The majority of lesions were described as
papular or urticarial (80%), with 78% of cases having associated
pruritus. Additional signs included associated petechiae (14%)
or purpura (2%), plaques (6%), burning sensation (2%), blanching
(2%), and pustules (2%). Interestingly, one case (2%) was
described as morbilliform in presentation. Systemic signs and
symptoms were less common and included fever (6%), diarrhea
(4%), paresthesia of the hands (2%), urticarial dermographism
(2%), mouth ulcer/mucosal involvement (4%), dysphagia (2%),
and conjunctivitis (2%).
Nearly all cases involved the trunk, and the majority of
patients had upper and/or lower limb involvement (70% and
66%, respectively). Involvement of the face and neck was less
common (22% and 12%, respectively), and only two or three
cases involved the hands and feet. The average time to onset
of symptoms was 42.4 h (range: 2-120 h) after the consumption
of shiitake mushrooms (Table 2).

question: Were all relevant study results collected for use in the synthesis?
answers:
Treatment
In general, shiitake dermatitis was self-limiting, and treatment
was focused on symptomatic management. A total of 31
patients were prescribed corticosteroids (topical, n = 14; systemic,
n = 8; not all studies specified which), and 21 patients
received antihistamines. Of those receiving treatment, 13 cases
were reported to have no relief of symptoms, and one patient
reported worsening of symptoms.17 Twelve patients received no
treatment. Other treatment approaches included polidocanol
and psoralen ultraviolet A (PUVA) therapy. One patient
attempted treatment with valacyclovir without relief, prior to a
diagnosis being established. Treatment modality was not
reported in two cases. All cases resulted in complete resolution
(two cases did not report outcomes or were lost from follow-up)
within an average of 10.74 d (range: 2-35 d; adequate data in
37 cases). The average time to resolution in cases not receiving
treatment was 12.5 d (range: 7-28 d; n = 8). In subjects receiving
steroid monotherapy, the average time to resolution was
11.2 d (range: 2-35 d; n = 19) in patients receiving topical formulations
and 9.3 d (range: 7-14 d; n = 4) in those receiving
systemic steroids. The mean time to resolution in subjects
receiving antihistamine and steroid combination therapy was
10.4 d (range: 2-21 d; n = 17). No cases reported antihistamine
monotherapy.

patients)

patients were prescribed corticosteroids (topical, n = 14; systemic,
n = 8; not all studies specified which), and 21 patients
received antihistamines.

question: Were the findings robust, e.g. as demonstrated through funnel plot or sensitivity analyses?
answers: nan

question: Were biases in primary studies minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers: nan

question: Did the interpretation of findings address all of the concerns identified in Domains 1 to 4?
answers:
Based on the case reports included, it appears that
the average time to clearance is shorter in patients treated with
systemic steroids (9.3 d; range: 7-14 d) compared with patients
who receive no treatment (12.5 d; range: 7-28 d). However, this
has not been substantiated by controlled trials, and these
reports are not sufficiently powered to warrant the recommendation of systemic steroids in all patients with shiitake dermatitis.
